note: EverComplant是一家收集在线数据情报，通过在线交易检测和防止洗钱的初创公司，该公司表示，它已经在B系列融资中筹集了3500万美元。该公司在一份声明中表示，筹集的资金将帮助这家初创公司满足银行、信用卡公司、支付处理商、支付服务商、在线市场和执法机构日益增长的需求。该公司还更名为EverC。总部位于以色列的早期成长阶段风险投资公司红点资本合伙公司(Red Dot Capital Partners)在这轮融资中处于领先地位。茂尔投资公司(Maor Investments)和该公司现有的支持者也加入了这轮融资，其中包括乔伊·洛(Joey Low)、Viola Ventures、Arbor Ventures和美国运通风险投资公司(American Express Ventures)。虽然在新冠肺炎之前，全球电子商务交易量已经在上升，但这场流行病加速了数字支付的采用，随之而来的是在线犯罪活动的增加。EverC在一份声明中表示，最近备受瞩目的案件只是突显了已经成为前所未有的在线洗钱水平。这家初创公司提供的数据显示，每年通过欺诈性电子商务交易洗钱约5000亿美元。此外，目前有600万至1000万在线商家在未经正在处理的金融机构知情或同意的情况下，使用现有的在线支付系统处理交易。EverC相信，其数据智能能力将有助于减少网络犯罪，并有助于推动电子商务增长。该公司使用人工智能来查看客户的投资组合，并利用其网络情报能力识别与潜在罪犯的联系。EverC联合创始人兼首席执行官罗恩·泰歇尔(Ron Teicher)表示：“许多类型的严重犯罪活动都试图隐藏在看起来合法的门面后面，这些门面很难被追踪和揭开面纱，数字技术让潜在的欺诈者更容易隐藏在众目睽睽之下。”他说：“我们相信光线是最好的消毒剂，在数码时代做生意，透明度和可见性是非常重要的。EverC渴望成为促进在线支付的组织的信心源泉，为他们提供驾驭风险和利用新机会所需的工具、数据和洞察力。“。“随着商业越来越多地在网上移动，与之相关的犯罪也在增加。EverC处于机器学习和人工智能研究的前沿，旨在开发解决史诗般的问题的技术，直接影响到我们所有人的福祉，“红点资本合伙公司(Red Dot Capital Partners)管理合伙人巴拉克·所罗门(Barak SalSolomon)表示。所罗门将加入EverC董事会。该公司由罗恩·泰歇尔(Ron Teicher)、诺姆·拉比诺维奇(Noam Rabinovich)和拉兹·阿布拉莫夫(Raz Abramov)于2008年创立，总部设在纽约市，在特拉维夫和上海设有办事处。声明称，EverC的产品MerchantView用于检测在线洗钱，MarketView用于保持市场不受非法产品的影响，这些产品正被金融机构和支付服务提供商使用，包括北美和南美的财富500强公司、EMEA和亚太地区。
note_en: EverCompliant, a startup that gleans online data intelligence to detect and prevent money laundering via online transactions, said it has raised a $35 million in Series B funding. The money raised will help the startup meet growing demand from banks, credit card companies, payment processors, payment facilitators, online marketplaces, and law enforcement agencies, the company said in a statement. The firm has also rebranded to EverC.    Red Dot Capital Partners, an Israel-based early growth stage venture capital firm, led the round. Maor Investments joined the round, as did the company’s existing backers, among them Joey Low, Viola Ventures, Arbor Ventures, and American Express Ventures.											  While the volume of global e-commerce transactions was already rising pre-COVID-19, the pandemic has accelerated the adoption of digital payments, and an increase in criminal activities online has followed in its wake. Recent high-profile cases have only highlighted what has already become an unprecedented level of online money laundering, EverC said in a statement. Figures provided by the startup show that some $500 billion is laundered annually through fraudulent e-commerce transactions. In addition, there are 6 to 10 million online merchants currently using existing online payment systems to process transactions without the knowledge or consent of the financial institution they are processing through. EverC believes its data intelligence capabilities will help reduce online crime and help boost e-commerce growth. The firm uses artificial intelligence to look into its client’s portfolios and identify links with potential criminals using its cyber intelligence capabilities. “Many types of serious criminal activities are trying to hide behind legitimate-looking facades that are very difficult to track and unmask, digital technology has made it easier for potential fraudsters to hide in plain sight,” said Ron Teicher, EverC’s co-founder and CEO. “We believe that light is the best disinfectant and transparency and visibility are critical in doing business in the digital age. EverC aspires to be a source of confidence for organizations who facilitate payments online, providing them the tools, data, and insights needed to navigate risk and capitalize on new opportunities.” “As commerce moves increasingly online so does the crime associated with it. EverC is at the forefront of machine learning and AI research to develop technology that addresses a problem of epic magnitude, with a direct impact on the wellbeing of all of us,” said Barak Salomon, managing partner at Red Dot Capital Partners. Salomon is joining EverC’s board. Founded in 2008 by Ron Teicher, Noam Rabinovich and Raz Abramov, the  firm is headquartered in New York City with offices in Tel Aviv and Shanghai. EverC’s products, MerchantView for detecting online money laundering and MarketView for keeping marketplaces clean from illegal products, are being used by the financial institutions and payment service providers, including Fortune 500 companies in North and South America, EMEA and Asia Pacific, the statement said.
title: EverC为银行和支付处理器提供支持，以检测和防止在线洗钱，该公司在红点资本合伙公司(Red Dot Capital Partners)牵头下筹集了3500万美元的B系列资金
title_en: EverC, which provides banks and payment processors support to detect and prevent online money laundering, raises $35M Series B led by Red Dot Capital Partners

微软今年庆祝两个主要里程碑：Windows95几周前迎来25岁生日，双屏Surface二人组标志着微软向移动领域的盛大回归，这是微软被迫向苹果和谷歌投降的竞争战场。
这两项成就讲述了两家截然不同的公司的故事。一个被困在狭隘的愿景中，另一个则采取大胆的赌博，在首席执行官萨蒂亚·纳德拉的北极星的领导下，推动每个人--不仅仅是自己--前进，“帮助人们取得更多成就”。
尽管微软仍然被广泛认为是Windows公司或Office公司，但它已经悄悄地将自己重塑为一家向前设计的创新者，奇怪的是，它在一个高度两极分化的时刻发出了团结的声音。技术曾经是极客专家的领域，现在已经完全成为主流。硬件、软件和服务-对于大多数消费者来说，它们的外观和工作方式都是一样的。IPhone就是iPhone，iOS和Android现在毫无歉意地互相抄袭对方的功能。
在另一个世界，靠边站的微软可能会简单地接受苹果和谷歌的失败，退回到阴影中。但即将上任的首席执行官萨蒂亚·纳德拉(Satya Nadella)将公司及其高管团队转变为没人预料到的事情。纳德拉颁布了一项新使命，即在用户的平台上、在非Windows设备上使用其服务来满足用户；他打破了前任们的固执，即让Windows成为一个对平台无关的方法至关重要的主导平台。纳德拉培养了一种开放的政策，而不是使用咄咄逼人的策略来垄断开发商和垄断市场。他还停止了延续乏味、乏味的设计--这是微软公司成立之初的遗留问题。
在纳德拉的领导下，微软本可以完全专注于企业-永远销售Office Suite服务，Azure云计算，并促进与GitHub等社区更好的开发者关系。但这不会让针头移动-至少不会在前端移动到消费者手中。
微软真正的转变是愿意承担大胆的风险-滑向冰球要去的地方，而不是它现在或曾经去过的地方。虽然微软之前过于专注于世界主导地位，看不到移动设备的崛起，但纳德拉的政策实际上已经使该公司再次成为一家强大的公司-更仁慈的公司。他的欢迎立场让首席产品官帕诺斯·帕奈(Panos Panay)等铁杆人物能够用Surface设备生产出漂亮的硬件设计。它创造了吸引人的实用软件，通过其流利的设计语言和备受欢迎的产品，如iOS上的Outlook和基于Chromium的Edge Web浏览器，可以看到这一点。新的微软还接受了易访问的社交、交流和协作工具，如LinkedIn、Xbox和团队。
有了这两个人-在这里阅读我们的完整评论-微软正在利用其从创造文字和形象转换体验(Surface、Windows Ink、OneNote等)中学到的所有东西。以引入新的外形规格。从根本上说，这两个人是一部电话。然而，它的两个屏幕让它变得更加强大：它是一台平板电脑、一本书、一台迷你笔记本电脑，就像Surface Pro或Surface Studio一样，这两个人并不局限于一种单一的模式(也就是潘奈所说的“姿势”)。它也是令人难以置信的华丽--是一款真正的高级感觉设备。
“这个产品会适应你们的，”Panay在与微软团队的视频通话中告诉我。“意思是，选择你想要使用的外形，让它唱出来。这就是挑战所在。“
我发现微软开发新技术产品的方式令人难以置信地着迷，因为尽管我非常喜欢背诵和比较速度和馈送，但它带来的变革性体验最终才是最重要的。无论您身在何处，该设备的外观和手感是否良好？二人组的两个屏幕真的能帮我做更多吗？节省时间？这两款产品实现了哪些典型智能手机无法实现的使用案例？
二人组正在转型的硬件和软件提供了一种新的体验，可能会也可能不会与刚走出大门的人联系在一起。(微软只有在尝试之后才会知道。)。但它的发布也象征着一个新的微软，它明白光靠好的软件或硬件是不够的。正是这种组合让产品变得强大。
在微软，没有人比潘内更了解这个神奇的公式，他和蔼可亲地从假期中抽出时间与家人谈论他的孩子二人组。
输入：DUO是一种新的外形规格。它表面上是一部电话。Duo的目标是什么？在Windows Mobile(Née Windows Phone)失败后，如何赢回移动业务？
帕诺斯：你喜欢的是微软，你了解的是安卓。我们的目标是：如何在移动设备上实现最佳的微软体验？重塑移动软件平台并不是一件容易的事。它是让微软在你口袋里的东西上变得伟大。它根本没有把[Windows]甩在后面，它正在采用更多。归根结底，设备是我们构建的所有东西的端点。
我相信这是一件变革性的作品，正好落在帮助人们取得更多成就这一使命的中间。我们现在就需要这个。今天你口袋里的手机是有局限性的。它太棒了，你可以用它做很多事情，它们是非常酷的设备，我就住在其中一台上面。或者我曾经有过。
我并不是说它们不起作用。他们令人难以置信，但这是下一步，这是一个机会，让人们以不同的方式参与进来，以一种新的方式在情感上建立联系，然后做更多的事情。我们如何帮助人们取得更多成就-这是我们的使命。它存在于我们的灵魂之中。这是我们的信念。
输入：你们为什么不试着把软件做得更像Windows呢？没有让您想起Windows Mobile的动态磁贴。你们只是想从不愉快的记忆中彻底分手吗？
帕诺斯：你可以感觉到窗户洒在那里。您在底部确实有任务栏的外观，并且在您的前面确实有窗口。但归根结底，这并不是要脱离历史。这不是要试着叫它不同的东西。它是关于“用户现在需要什么？”
真正的设计要点是：不要试图重新创建Android。我们并不是试图篡改代码，创建一个全新的基础来让年长的手机用户记住一些东西。不是这样的。那就是他们现在需要什么？人们现在在哪里？并在客户所在的地方与他们会面。不要重新发明那些已经在为人们工作的东西。
输入：我很高兴你们正在拥抱其他平台，并在用户所在的地方与他们会面。不会为坚持使用Windows而感到如此自豪。
帕诺斯：归根结底，我们希望客户能够完成更多任务。当我说客户时，我指的是人-我们称之为终端用户池。最终，如果有人喜欢他们拿到的东西，比如当你拿起你的设备时，希望你会觉得“哇，当我拿起这个的时候，也许我感觉有点不同。”这里面有一些情绪化的东西。
这才是应该有联系的地方。归根结底，这种情绪就是硬件和软件走到了一起。
输入：很多人，特别是已经使用了一段时间的人，都把这款设备看作是Courier的概念。我想你们还没有正式说过二人组是从这个概念演变而来的。这两个人是出于建造这样的东西的需要而诞生的吗？那是10年前的事了。
帕诺斯：我当然尊重[快递概念的历史]。但我不是其中的一部分。我当时并没有在做这件事。事实上，一点也不。我和它没有关系。但我认为，不管(信使团队)在想什么--因为我不是那个团队的成员--我们最终知道，你会感觉到，两块屏幕能为你做什么的想法是真实的。大脑如何在这些窗口内工作，以及它们如何相互作用是相当强大的。找到它，理解它，并确定它是你的，这需要时间。
这个问题我已经被问过几次了：那么这就是信使？你终于完成了，对吧？我不想无礼地说不，因为微软有一个传统，而那从来就不是一个真正的产品。
帕诺斯：我们当时就知道--我想说是六年前，但我不确定，可能是五年前--我们有这样的概念：“如果手机足够薄，可以装进你的口袋，有两块屏幕，它的威力将是巨大的，这就是窗口的威力。”
我们知道大脑是怎么启动的。这是多显示器，但如果它在你的口袋里，它带来了所有的移动属性，这意味着它的轻薄、电池续航时间、速度、即时开机-所有你在移动平台上习惯的东西，会怎么样呢？最后，当然，你可以说Android为我们解决了非常好的应用程序。
我口袋里真的有一块金属坐在会议上。两块金属和我们发明的这个漂亮的铰链。它不像你现在用的那样结实。这只是一个概念，看看它是否适合我的口袋。有没有烦躁的因素？这个想法是真的吗？我会去这里吗？我会在这里用吗？这就是开始创建形式和功能的原因。
我们认为，如果他们天衣无缝地走到一起，这里就有可以改变的魔力。可以肯定的是，公司会去这里。不久之后，我们开始考虑折叠式屏幕，我们正在考虑一大堆其他的东西，下了管道，我们就会看到它们在问：限制是什么？(译者注：折叠式屏幕的限制是什么？)。你能把它放进口袋里吗？它起作用了吗？它真的能打开大脑吗？为了开始做他们正在做的事情，对人们来说，重新组织一切是不是太繁重了？所有这些概念都在滚滚而过。
帕诺斯：当我们制造产品时，我看到的是：你有技术。我们有什么线索？我们能做什么？你有用户需求，这是这里最重要的部分。然后你就有了优雅。科技和优雅往往交融在一起，但它们很重要。在这个时代，他们现在真的很重要。从根本上说，当你看到这三样东西结合在一起时，你就知道你拥有了一些东西。
输入：当我拿起这款设备时，我心里想，会有观众看到这两个人的不足之处。没有5G，没有厚挡板，没有无线充电，等等。你对这些人有什么要说的吗？
帕诺斯：有一群偏爱技术的观众，我知道我不会让每个人都满意。我不是想追逐技术规格。我从来都不是。这个产品，如果你试图追逐一个技术规格，你是对的，它会有可形成的可靠性问题。它上面会有一些根本不需要的东西，而且没有任何意义。但就其本身而言，它会牺牲掉你所需要的所有其他东西。
我希望每个人都能看到这一点。我确实读过我在Twitter之类的东西上的评论，它们就像是，你在想什么？等你把眼睛放在屏幕上，等你把手放到产品上再进去，难道你不知道现代手机是什么样子吗？
[在这一点上，帕诺斯真的是兴奋又书呆子，不是从傲慢的地方，而是从知识的角度]。
我绝对知道什么是现代电话！事实上，如果你愿意的话，我可以背诵这个星球上的每一条该死的规范。我可以告诉你技术是从哪里来的。我可以告诉你制造商是谁。我可以告诉你你是怎么做的。我可以告诉你挑战在哪里。
然而，我并不是要追逐每一个人。我想让人们知道，如果你能找到优雅地满足人们需求的绝对时刻，他们会取得更多成就。这很简单。
输入：实现二人组的最大挑战是什么？是铰链还是软件的问题？你们这些家伙痴迷于铰链。我刚重播了一段你们谈论铰链的老视频，然后把它做对了。
帕诺斯：当你拿起产品时，我们必须克服一些硬件限制，才能保证产品的完形效果。是铰链的问题吗？是软件的问题吗？实际上，把这两样东西放在一起。
知道你处于什么姿势，预测你将把它带到哪里，了解硬件和软件之间的缝隙，这样才是铰链和铰链角度，这就是软件，以及它是如何反应的。如果您将其展开[将应用程序全屏显示在两个显示屏上]，并将其旋转到您处于新闻馈送模式的位置，那么让我们确保我们以尽可能智能的方式将其提供给您。当你同时启动两个[APP]屏幕时，你已经把它移到了横向而不是纵向，你想要实现什么？当你把它折回去接电话或用两个手指打字时，键盘适应得好不好？所有这些都是在让用户选择的同时进行的。
如果您现在使用键盘，我们会将键盘移动到您的拇指下方或您的两个拇指下方，或者将其移动到横向模式。但实际上，只需点击一下按钮，您就可以改变这一点。创造适应性，让产品知道你在哪里--这一点很重要。但是要确保功能是正确的，但我不想这样，我想以这种方式使用它，这是适合您的产品的一部分。
给用户这样的选择，顺便说一句，这就是Android真正强大的地方。因为现在您有机会以常规状态或常规形式使用它，或者您想要将两个屏幕用作单个屏幕，我们可以这样做。我们可以为您创建它。这不是硬件调整，而是软件调整以适应用户。
输入：打开二人组，想要像笔记本一样在上面写字，感觉很自然。没有内置的Surface Pen。它是单独出售的。为什么没有Surface钢笔呢？就连我妈妈也认为应该有一支触控笔。
帕诺斯：当我们像这样推动一款设备向前发展时，什么时候才是整合钢笔深度的合适时机？我们现在在Office和OneNote中所做的一切-当你说你喜欢的微软-正在让笔变得栩栩如生。它只会继续进化，变得更好。
外形因素确实鼓励使用钢笔。在装有[Windows Ink]的设备上安装OneNote可能是你能找到的最好的延迟，或者是你在小尺寸栏上找不到的最好的写作感觉。我们花了过多的时间来确保优雅完美，因为我们希望确保在OneNote上记录您正在做的任何事情的体验都很棒。
放下墨水的想法是完美的。今天我们只关注如何让微软套件与笔完美地配合工作，然后随着它的发展，你会继续看到更多与笔的集成。我们现在有一些应用程序开发人员正在使用API，正在为Ink编写应用程序或增强您的应用程序，因为他们希望他们的单屏幕二人组Ink体验非常棒-这对我们来说是令人兴奋的。我们不会放松这一点的。我们都参与了这件事。这是生产力体验的一部分。Duo和Surface Slim Pen将并排销售。
最终，您开始参与设计决策。您想在什么时候将钢笔集成到产品中？您想要多薄的产品？主要使用案例有哪些？第二级和第三级用例有哪些？当你开始了解这一点时，你就会开始明白这就是客户现在需要的东西。让我们给他们这一点，让我们确保钢笔在那里，帮助他们到达他们想去的地方。
然后在未来，那就是我们下一步要做什么？它带来的只是一个很棒的比较，你知道，我们下一步做什么的很棒的一块？
输入：最后一个问题。你害怕二人组失败吗？您是否担心或担心这两款产品和这款双屏产品可能不会成为其他公司效仿的参考设计？如果它不能像Surface Pro那样改变游戏规则呢？
帕诺斯：我不会认为这是第一代设备。我们可能没有推出前两代产品，但您无法在第一代产品上实现这种外形规格。你只要做就行了。有人说过，帕诺斯需要你三个氏族，我就是这么说的。这是个好消息，因为我现在正在研发这款产品的第三代产品。我们没有发货，但我们已经研究了很多年了。微软增长思维的一部分是从失败中学习。但你不一定非得发货才会失败。
你必须制造产品。你必须到达你所说的有限制的地方，现在不是时候。我们多年来一直在用这种产品做到这一点。我们现在在这里。我害怕吗？不，不。我对学习感到兴奋吗？绝对一点儿没错。我相信我们会在人们使用这个产品的时候学习吗？是啊。我知道吗？就像我告诉你的那样，从一部小手机到一部大手机，或者从一个操作系统到另一个操作系统，都有学习曲线吗？是的，有。
我说这话时不能听起来像什么奇怪的傲慢。这个团队相信你在这里可以做的更多。我们的路线图就在我们面前，我们可以看到。我们会看到产品随着时间的推移会去哪里。我们知道，我们现在手中的就是目前的权利。我们都知道。我相信其他人也会效仿的。这些产品需要时间才能被采用。
他说：“我们知道，我们现在手中的东西目前是正确的。我相信其他人也会效仿。“。
当然，如果我不相信这一点，你会说：你在做什么？归根结底，如果出现更多的外形因素，这对我来说是一件令人惊叹的事情，因为我们的软件和硬件集成变成了回到用户需求的东西。回到萨蒂亚的观点上来，不要害怕采用其他平台。它不一定非得是我的硬件或这个团队的基础软件。
但是，你可以在全球范围内使用我们所有的服务和应用程序套件--无论是谁创建的--基于这些新的外形因素--我认为这太棒了。然后你会说，如果它能帮助人们取得更多成就，那我就是其中的一员。
note_en:
Microsoft celebrates two major milestones this year: Windows 95 turned 25 a few weeks ago and the  dual-screen Surface Duo marks Microsoft’s grand return to mobile, a competitive battleground it was forced to surrender to Apple and Google.
 The two achievements tell the story of two very different companies. One trapped in tunnel vision, and the other taking bold gambles to push everyone — not just itself — forward under CEO Satya Nadella’s North Star to “help people achieve more.”
 Though still widely considered The Windows Company or The Office Company, Microsoft has quietly reinvented itself as a design-forward innovator and, strangely enough, a voice of unity in a highly polarized moment. Once the domain of geek-experts, technology is now utterly mainstream. Hardware, software, and services — they all look and work the same for most consumers.  An iPhone is an iPhone and iOS and Android now  unapologetically copy each other’s features.
 In an alternate universe, a sidelined Microsoft would have simply accepted defeat by Apple and Google and retreated into the shadows. But incoming-CEO Satya Nadella pivoted the company and its executive team into something no one saw coming. Nadella decreed a new mission to meet users with its services on  their platforms, on non-Windows devices; he broke with his predecessors’ fixations of making Windows the one dominant platform that matters for a platform agnostic approach. Nadella fostered an open door policy instead of using aggressive tactics to corner developers and monopolize markets. He also stopped perpetuating dull, tasteless design — a holdover from Microsoft’s corporate beginnings.
 Under Nadella, Microsoft could have focused completely on enterprise — forever selling Office Suite services, Azure cloud computing, and promoting better developer relations with communities like  GitHub. But that wouldn’t have moved the needle — not on the front-end to consumers at least.
   Microsoft’s real transformation has been its willingness to take bold and daring risks — skate to where the puck is going, not where it is or has been. Whereas Microsoft previously was too occupied with world dominance to see mobile’s rise, Nadella’s policies have actually turned the company into a powerhouse again — a more benevolent one, that is. His welcoming stance empowered stalwarts like chief product officer Panos Panay to produce beautiful hardware design with the Surface devices. It’s created attractive  and utilitarian software seen through its  Fluent design language and beloved products like Outlook on iOS and the Chromium-based Edge web browser. The new Microsoft has also embraced accessible social, communicative, and collaborative tools like LinkedIn, Xbox, and Teams.
 With the Duo —  read our full review here — Microsoft is leveraging all of its learnings from creating literal and figurative transforming experiences (Surface, Windows Ink, OneNote, etc.) to usher in a new form factor. On a basic level, the Duo is a phone. However, its two screens make it so much more: it’s a tablet, a book, a mini laptop, Like a  Surface Pro or a  Surface Studio, the Duo is not limited to a singular mode (or “posture” as Panay calls it).  It's also incredibly gorgeous — a real premium feeling device.
  “The product’s gonna adapt to you,” Panay told me over a video call on Microsoft Teams. “Meaning, pick the form factor you want to use it in and let it sing. That’s the challenge.”
  I find Microsoft’s approach to new technology products incredibly fascinating because as much as I enjoy reciting and comparing speeds and feeds, the transformative experience it enables is all that matters at the end of the day.  Does the device look and feel good to use wherever you are? Can the Duo’s two screens really help me do more? Save time? What use cases does the Duo enable that a typical smartphone can’t?
 The Duo’s transforming hardware and software offer a new experience that may or may not connect with people right out the gate. (Microsoft won’t know until it tries.) But its release is also symbolic of a new Microsoft that understands neither good software or hardware alone is enough. It’s the combination that makes a product potent.
 No person at Microsoft understands this magical formula better than Panay, who graciously took time out of his vacation with his family to talk shop about his baby, the Duo.
  Input: The Duo is a new form factor. It’s ostensibly a phone. What is the goal with Duo? To win back mobile after Windows Mobile (née Windows Phone) failed?
 Panos: It's the Microsoft you love and the Android you know. The goal was: how do you make the best Microsoft experience possible on a mobile device? It wasn't to reinvent the mobile software platform. It was make Microsoft great on the thing that’s in your pocket. It's not leaving [Windows] behind at all, it's adopting more. At the end of the day, the device is an endpoint for all things we build.
 I do believe this is that transformative piece that lands right in the middle of that mission of helping people achieve more. We need that right now. The phone in your pocket today has limitations. It's awesome and you can do a ton with it and they're freaking cool devices and I live on one of them. Or I had.
 I'm not saying they don't work. They're incredible but this is the next step and it is an opportunity for people to engage differently, emotionally connect in a new way, and then get more done. How do we help people achieve more — which is our mission. It's in our soul. It's our belief.
  Input: Why didn’t you guys try to make the software more Windows-like? There are no Live tiles that reminds you of Windows Mobile. Did you guys just want a clean break from the bad memories?
 Panos: You can feel Windows sprinkled in there. You do have the semblance of a taskbar at the bottom and you do have the windowing in front of you. But at the end of the day, it wasn’t about separating from history. It wasn’t about trying to call it something different. It was about “what do users need right now?”
 The true design point was: do not try to recreate Android. We’re not trying to fork the code and create a whole new base to give older phone users something they remember. That wasn’t it. It was what do they need now? Where are people now? And meeting customers where they are. Don't reinvent things that are already working for folks.
  Input: I like that you guys are embracing other platforms and meeting users where they are. Not being so prideful about sticking with Windows.
 Panos: At the end of the day, we want customers to be able to accomplish more. When I say customers, I mean people — we call it end-user pool. Where ultimately, if somebody loves what they pick up, like when you picked up your device, hopefully, you felt like “whoa, when I pick this up, maybe I feel a bit different.” There’s something emotional about it.
 That’s what the connection is supposed to be. At the end of the day, that emotion is the hardware and software coming together.
  Input: A lot of people, especially people who’ve been around for a while, have seen this device as the Courier concept. I don’t think you guys have ever formally said the Duo is an evolution from that concept. Was the Duo born out of the need to build something like that? That was 10 years ago.
 Panos: I respect [the history of the Courier concept] for sure. But I wasn't part of it. I wasn’t working on it at the time. Actually, at all. I wasn’t connected to it. But I think at the end of the day knowing, regardless of what [the Courier team] was thinking — because I wasn't in the ethos of that team — we do know you're gonna feel that this idea of what two screens can do for you is real. How the brain works within these windows and how they can interact with one another is pretty powerful. It takes time to find it, and understand it, and make it yours for sure.
 I've been asked this question a few times: So this is Courier? You finally got it done, right? I don't want to be disrespectful and say no because there's a heritage there in Microsoft and that was never a real product.
   Panos: We knew at the time — I'm gonna say six years ago but I don't know for sure, five years ago maybe — we had this concept of “if the phone could ever be thin enough and fit in your pocket with two screens, the power of that would be immense and that was the power of windowing.”
 We knew the way the brain turned on. This is multi-monitor, but what if it was in your pocket and it brought all the mobile attributes with it which meant thinness, battery life, speed, instant on — all the things that you’re used to on a mobile platform? And then ultimately, of course, apps which you can argue Android solved tremendously well for us.
 I literally had this piece of metal in my pocket sitting in meetings. Two pieces of metal together with this beautiful hinge that we had invented. It wasn't robust like the one you're using now. It was a concept just to see if it fit in my pocket. Was there a fidget factor? Was this idea real? Would I go here? And would I use it here? That’s what started to create the form and function.
 We thought if they came together seamlessly, there's magic here that can transform. For sure companies will go here. Shortly after, we're looking at folding screens, we're looking at a bunch of other things, and coming down the pipe we're looking at them going: What are the limitations? Can you put it in your pocket? Does it work? Does it really turn the brain on? Is it too much work for people to reorganize everything to start doing what they're doing? All these concepts were rolling through.
  Panos: What I look at when we're building products: you have technology. What do we have? What can be done? You have user needs, which is the biggest piece here. And then you have elegance. Technology and elegance tend to blend, but they matter. They really do matter now in this day and age. Fundamentally, when you see those three things come together, you know you have something.
  Input: When I picked up this device, I thought to myself there’s going to be an audience that sees deficiencies in the Duo. There’s no 5G, it’s got thick bezels, there’s no wireless charging, etc. What do you have to say to these people?
 Panos: There's a tech-leaning audience and I know I'm not gonna make everybody happy. I'm not trying to chase a tech spec. I never was. This product, if you tried to chase a tech spec, you are right, it would have formable reliability problems. It would have things on it that are just not needed and don't make any sense. But as such it would have traded off everything else that you needed.
 I hope everybody can see that. I do read my comments on Twitter or stuff and they're like, "What were you thinking?" Wait till you put your eyes on the screen, wait till you put your hands on the product before you get into, "Don't you know what a modern phone is like?"
 [At this point, Panos is really excited and nerdy, not from a place of arrogance, but from knowledge]
 I absolutely know what a modern phone is! As a matter of fact, I can recite every effing spec on the planet if you'd like me to. I can tell you where the tech comes from. I can tell you who the manufacturers are. I can tell you how you make them. I can tell you where the challenges are.
 However, I'm not trying to chase every one of those. What I want is for people to know that if you can find this absolute moment of meeting people's needs with elegance, they're going to achieve more. It's pretty simple.
   Input: What was the single most challenging of making the Duo happen? Was it the hinge or the software? You guys are obsessed with hinges. I just rewatched an old video of you guys talking about hinges and getting that right.
 Panos: There are hardware limitations that we had to overcome to keep the gestalt to the product when you pick it up. Was it the hinge? Was it the software? Actually, put those two things together.
 Knowing what posture you're in, predicting where you're taking it, understanding that seam between the hardware and software, so that's the hinge and the hinge angle, that's the software and how it's reacting. If you span it [spread an app fullscreen across both displays] and rotate it where you’re in the newspaper feed mode, so let's make sure we give it to you in the smartest way possible. When you launch two [app] screens at the same time and you've moved it to landscape versus portrait, what were you trying to accomplish? When you fold it back to take a call or do two-finger typing finger typing, did the keyboard adapt perfectly or not? All while letting the user choose.
 If you use the keyboard as it is today, we move the keyboard under your thumb or under both your thumbs or we move it to landscape mode. But actually, with one click of the button you can change that. Creating the adaptability so the product knew where you were — that was important. But then making sure the functionality was "but I don't want that, I want to use it this way," which is part of the product adapting to you.
 Giving the user that choice, that's where Android's really powerful by the way. Because now you have this opportunity to use it in a regular state or regular form, or you want to use both screens as a single screen, we can do it. We can create that for you. That's not the hardware adapting, that's the software adapting to the user.
  Input: It feels natural to open the Duo and want to write on it just like a notebook. There’s no built-in Surface Pen. It’s sold separately. Why isn’t there a Surface Pen? Even my mom thinks there should be a stylus.
 Panos: When we're pushing a device forward like this, when is the right time to integrate the depth of a pen? Everything we're doing right now in Office and OneNote — when you say the Microsoft you love — is bringing the pen to life. And it will only continue to evolve to get better.
 The form factor does encourage pen. OneNote on the device with [Windows Ink] is probably the best latency you're gonna find or the best writing feeling you're gonna find anywhere on a small form factor bar none. We spent an inordinate amount of time making sure that the elegance was perfect because we wanted to make sure the note-taking experience on OneNote on whatever you were doing was great.
  The idea that laying Ink down is perfect. We have a very narrow focus today on getting the Microsoft suite working perfectly with the pen and then as it evolves you will continue to see more integration with the pen. We have app developers right now that are taking the APIs and are writing apps or enhancing your apps for Ink because they want their single screen Duo Ink experience to be great — that's exciting for us. We're not going to let up on that. We're all in on it. It's part of the productivity experience. Duo and the Surface Slim Pen will be sold side by side.
 Ultimately, you start getting into design decisions. When do you want to integrate the pen into the product? How thin do you want the product to be? What are the primary use cases? What are the secondary and tertiary use cases? When you start getting into that, you start understanding here's what the customer needs now. Let's give them that and let's make sure the pens are there to help them get to where they want to go.
 And then in the future, it's what do we do next? what it brings is just a great comp, you know, great piece of what do we do next?
 Input: Last question. Are you afraid of the Duo failing? Are you worried or concerned that the Duo and this dual-screen form factor might not be the reference design for others to follow? What if it doesn't end up being a game changer like the Surface Pro?
 Panos: I wouldn't think about this as a gen one device. We may not have shipped the first two generations, but you can't get to this form factor on a gen one. You just can't. Someone had said "Panos, it'll take you three gens and I'm like that's good news because I'm now on the third-gen of this product." We didn't ship it, but we've been working on it for years. Part of the growth mindset of Microsoft is learning from failure. But you don't have to ship to fail.
 You have to build the products. You have to get to where you say there's a limitation and this is not the right time. We've done that with this product for years. We're now here. Am I afraid? No, no, no. Am I excited to learn? Absolutely. Do I believe that we will learn when people use this product? Yep. Do I know that there's a learning curve like I told you from a small phone to a big phone or from one OS to another? Yeah, there is.
  I gotta say this without sounding like some weird arrogant. This team believes there's more you can do here. We have our roadmap in front of us, we can see it. We see where the product goes over time. We know that what we have in hand now is right for now. We know that. I believe that others will follow suit. These products take time for adoption.
 “We know that  what we have in hand now is right for  now. I believe that others will follow suit.”
 Of course, if I didn't believe that, you'd be like: what are you doing? At the end of the day, if more form factors come, that's amazing for me because our software and hardware integration becomes something that goes back to what users need. Back to Satya's point "don't be afraid to adopt other platforms." It doesn't have to be my hardware or this team's fundamental software.
 But the idea that you can use all our suite of services and applications worldwide — no matter who builds it — on these new form factors — I think that's awesome. Then you say "if it helps people achieve more" that's what I'm here to be part of.
title: 与Panos Panay问答关于Duo的起源，为什么它上的软件不像Windows一样，没有内置Surface Pen，也没有追逐技术规格
title_en: Q&A with Panos Panay about the origins of Duo, why the software on it is not Windows-like, the absence of a built-in Surface Pen, and not chasing tech specs

note:
跳到主要内容如果您从Verge链接购买内容，Vox Media可能会赚取佣金。请参阅我们的道德声明。
微软的Surface Duo渴望成为与你使用过的任何其他小工具不同的东西。它可能会被误认为手机或小平板电脑，但它既不比这些东西多，也比这些东西多。
当它关闭时，没有屏幕或摄像头。在形状和外观上，它就像一本小本子，是我放在后袋里随身携带的Field Notes笔记本的数字版本。打开时，您会看到两个并排的屏幕，或者一个屏幕折叠起来，另一个向后折叠。而且，就像笔记本一样，拿着它感觉比手机的垂直平板形状更自然。在某种程度上，它比手机的垂直平板形状更自然。你把它拿在手里，两边像一本书一样对着你。
事实上，Surface Duo没有什么是你目前的智能手机或平板电脑做不到的。你的智能手机肯定能拍出更好的照片，而你的平板电脑中间也没有太大的差距。但Surface二人组的不同之处在于，你做事的方式是独一无二的。我发现自己在处理那些在其他设备上会让我沮丧的任务。
微软对Surface二人组的起价为1399美元。考虑到它相对于其他手机的性能，它绝对不值得这个价格。即使在几周后，我也不完全相信Surface Duo真的比任何标准的单屏手机都更有效率。但是，即使并不总是正确的，有时在二人组中同时处理多项任务会产生更少的精神摩擦。和二人组在一起时，我经常感觉更有效率。
Surface Duo是一个可爱的、设计精良的物体。它的每一毫米显然都是带有意图的-尽管有时这种意图过于集中，可能会导致恼人的妥协。
关于二人组的硬件，最重要的是它的厚度：打开时为4.8毫米，关闭时为9.9毫米。微软对纤薄的执着使这两个人成为一款袖珍的符合人体工程学的设备。对于有两个屏幕和两个电池的设备来说，它也相对较轻，只有250克。
Surface二人组的轻薄是其吸引力的关键，就像微软保持外部没有屏幕或摄像头凹凸不平的事实一样。这些选择让它变得更薄，拿起来也很舒服，但它们也发出了一个信号，表明这是一种不同的设备。
像打开一本书一样打开这台设备，然后像一本书一样合上它，这种感觉与解锁智能手机有着根本的不同。在这两种情况下，它都会让你处于不同的心态。设计的意向性鼓励您有意使用设备，有意不使用它。
铰链是经典的Surface，也就是说，它是精心设计的，感觉很棒。它可以旋转360度，这样你就可以绕着后面旋转其中一个屏幕，像使用传统手机一样使用它。或者你可以把一边向上倾斜，当你打视频电话时，它会保持原地不动。它坚固而不僵硬，所有这些紧凑的公差都将屏幕之间的间隙降至最小。
它的正面和背面都是玻璃的--正面和背面，我想我应该说。他们被夹在金属栏杆周围。滑轨上有USB-C端口，但没有耳机插孔。这一次，认为手机太薄不适合耳机插孔的论点说得通。你还可以在那里找到音量按钮、电源按钮和指纹传感器(如果你问我，它们应该集成到电源按钮中，但事实并非如此)。
不是每件事都是完美的。如果我非常仔细地观察，我可以看到铰链上有一个微小的不对称，甚至可能沿着顶部和底部有最轻微的弯曲。它还配备了塑料保险杠，这有助于在边缘相当尖锐的角落，也与抓地力。我的阅读器上的两个显示屏之间也有很小的颜色差异-左侧的白点看起来有点红。直到我把灯调暗看一本Kindle书，我才注意到这一点。
但白点并不是对硬件的主要抱怨。我会把它留到双5.6英寸OLED屏幕上方和下方的边框上。它们很大，我认为它们是那些恼人的妥协之一，为了让二人组变得如此瘦小，这些妥协是必要的。与大多数挡板、凹槽和其他屏幕边框一样，您会习惯使用它们。
除此之外，它们都是很好的屏幕。它们在明亮的光线下可读，像素密集，并且有很好的视角。如果您有Surface Pen，它也可以在这个屏幕上使用。然而，它们没有1000美元以上手机上越来越标准的高刷新率。
这些屏幕也比最近任何一款智能手机都要宽得多。每一款都设计有4：3的纵横比，这意味着你不能用一只手使用-即使对折也不能使用。就像二人组的其他设计一样，我认为宽度是为了潜移默化地影响你对这款设备的看法。你的应用程序在外观上和感觉上都更像桌面上的窗口，而不是你最终会滚动的列表。
许多其他东西也被牺牲在了薄的祭坛上：NFC，无线充电，更大的电池，可扩展的存储，5G，也许还有更现代的规格，比如更快的处理器和更大的RAM。只有一个扬声器，而且没有很大的震动。但所有这些你在这种价位的手机上所期待的东西理论上都是可以消费的，因为Surface二人组理论上不是一部手机，而是完全不同的东西。
实现这一承诺的责任落在软件身上，它将决定整个事情的成败。
微软可能用Surface Duo上的软件吃了很多东西。它发送给评论家的原始版本充满了令人望而却步的错误和崩溃。不到一周前，该公司推出了一个软件更新-与今天将在零售设备上发布的更新相同-解决了相当大比例的这些漏洞。
但有些细菌仍然存在。相机应用程序存在输入滞后和错失的麻烦，还有其他奇怪的问题，比如键盘无缘无故地跳入视线。如果除了软件稳定性没有其他原因，那么在考虑购买Surface Duo之前，等待更多人对他们的Surface Duo体验的评价是值得的。
令人惊讶的是，其中一个问题并不是对Android平板电脑应用程序不好的抱怨--这是长期存在的，也是当之无愧的。因为安卓平板电脑不多，许多安卓开发者理性地选择不开发针对平板电脑优化的应用程序版本，这意味着没有动力生产安卓平板电脑，情况就是这样。
在两个屏幕上运行两个应用程序通常比尝试在Android平板电脑上分屏运行要容易得多。
应用程序可用性问题可能会演变成恶性循环，这是微软从Windows Phone中非常清楚的问题。因此，Surface Duo上最聪明的软件设计之一就是微软选择不做的事情：制造平板电脑。相反，这两个人正如它的名字所暗示的：两个手机大小的屏幕主要是为了并行运行应用程序。
仅凭其本身，轻松启动并行应用程序的能力就非常棒。无需学习其他界面，您只需在每个屏幕上点击您想要的图标，然后将它们并排使用即可。这可能看起来不是你对手机感兴趣的东西，但可能是因为单屏幕太痛苦了。
帮助你在笔记本电脑上提高工作效率的一件事是Windows-带有应用程序框的界面，我的意思是，不是操作系统。有了windows可以更容易地交叉引用内容，并且在执行其他任务时不太可能丢失状态。而且，您可以将内容从一个窗口移动到另一个窗口，而不必与其他界面元素混为一谈。
但到目前为止，每一次在手机上复制这一点的尝试都是糟糕的。在Duo上，更好的原因很简单，因为在每个屏幕上都可以很容易地打开一个应用程序。微软曾试图兜售应用程序之间的拖放，但很难正确使用这种手势，而且它只在少数几个应用程序中起作用-所以值得庆幸的是，经过试验和真实的复制和粘贴可以很好地发挥作用。
铰链可以让你将两人的屏幕扭曲成微软所说的“姿势”。有观看电影的帐篷模式、翻转其中一个屏幕的单屏模式、带大键盘的模式和全屏模式。在全屏模式下，大多数应用程序都没有注意到屏幕之间的间隙的存在，因此内容要么看起来被愚蠢地分割了，要么甚至在OLED之间的负空间被切断了。
Kindle应用程序是将差距转化为优势的一个很好的例子：你每边都有一个页面，可以像拿一本小书一样握住这两个人。但除此之外，唯一能很好地处理这一差距的应用程序是(毫不奇怪的)微软自己的应用程序。特别是Outlook，它很棒：你的电子邮件列表在左边，电子邮件在右边。点击前进，原来的电子邮件就会移到左边，整个右边的屏幕就会变成你的新电子邮件的撰写窗口。这意味着当你逐点评论一封电子邮件时，你不必不断地上下滚动。
我喜欢的一个姿势是：大键盘。当你在有活动光标的情况下将Duo旋转90度时，底部屏幕会自动变成键盘，而主屏幕则会扩展到全景。它可以让您在更大的画布上看到您正在键入的内容，并为您提供一个非常大的键盘来引导。
但是，要让应用程序适合这些姿势并非易事，因为Android的自然状态是单一的手机屏幕。而这才是大多数错误真正开始出现的地方。
这也是一个遗憾，因为这些错误是微软在定制Android以实现真正的多任务体验时做出正确选择的结果。我不认为这对普通的观察者来说是显而易见的，但在花了十年的时间评估了几十种不同风格的Android之后，我可以肯定地说：微软在定制Android以满足其需求方面选择了可能最艰难的道路之一，但这是正确的道路。
与三星、LG和其他涉足安卓分屏和折叠屏版本的公司不同，微软试图制造一个多任务系统，看起来不像是附加在安卓更传统的界面之上。
虽然许多制造商坚持使用按钮，但现代版本的Android是通过从底部向上滑动进入操作系统的核心部分来工作的，比如多任务视图、主屏幕和你的应用程序。因此，微软已将该系统添加到该系统中。你可以向上滑动以获得一个应用程序窗口，然后在两个屏幕上四处拖动，以将其置于上述模式之一。
这是一个高尚的目标，但要记住所有的滑动都能做什么取决于你所处的环境，这是一个巨大的障碍。有时滑动会引发多任务处理，有时它会返回家中，有时它会启动应用程序抽屉，有时如果角度恰到好处(或错误)，它会将应用程序扔到另一个屏幕上。哦，如果你把二人组转向一边，别忘了UP现在已经在一边了。
这太多了，但这是一个可以学习的系统，我最终精通了它，甚至发现自己进入了Surface首席执行官帕诺斯·帕奈(Panos Panay)喜欢谈论的那种“流”状态。但我经常会进入愤怒状态，因为这两个人做了一些我没有预料到的事情，或者在我试图定位窗户时落后了。
所有这一切的真正耻辱是，微软已经实现了所有其他Android手机制造商(谷歌除外)的圣杯：一个全面、统一和连贯的应用和服务生态系统。如果你在微软的生态系统中，你将拥有Outlook、Office，与你的手机在Windows 10、OneNote和OneDrive上兼容，所有这些都可以和谐地协同工作。
这比你在三星或LG手机上找到的任何同类产品都要好得多。另外，如果你不想使用，微软不会强迫你使用。谷歌搜索是默认的，而不是Bing，Gmail就坐在那里，依此类推。
说到谷歌，当我把这些问题中的一些问题归咎于Android本身时，我是认真的。几年来，谷歌一直在兜售对可折叠功能的支持，但在很长一段时间里，它一直未能让Android平板电脑获得成功。这笔技术债务的结果是，Android对Surface Duo和三星Galaxy Z Fold 2等设备毫无准备。但归根结底，Surface Duo背后并不是谷歌的标志。这是微软的。
在这一切过程中，仍然有一些时刻感觉我在Duo上可以比在其他手机上做更多的事情。随着时间的推移，这些时刻变成了更长的时间段。
Surface二人组的相机是垃圾。我应该和你谈谈它的可取之处，比如它对色彩平衡有一种克制的看法，这一点我在使用三星过于生动的相机后很欣赏，或者你可以在相机应用程序的双屏幕上看到一边的自拍，同时用另一边拍照。可是，我不会呀。
这款相机很可能是微软为Surface二人组长期致力于瘦身的又一牺牲品。也许1100万像素的传感器就是最适合这个超薄机身的了。但如果说Google Pixel4A教会了我们什么的话，那就是好的软件可以用平庸的硬件创造奇迹。
相反，我体验到的唯一的奇迹就是想知道如何让相机在第一时间工作。因为它被放置在其中一个屏幕的内侧，所以自拍很容易。启动相机，拍照，对结果微笑。见鬼，微软甚至在良好的照明条件下也有一个可以通过的肖像模式-嗯，无论如何，按照2018年的标准来说是可以通过的。
但如果你想给别人拍照，你必须旋转另一个屏幕，这样它才能成为取景器。但当你试图这样做时，取景器有一半的可能性会站在错误的一边。把它放在一边是一种沮丧的字面上的练习。你必须不停地扭动和敲击二人组，直到它弄明白为止。在所有这些工作之后，结果是泥泞的，嘈杂的，在昏暗的光线下，它们是一团糟。另外，它们在你按下快门按钮之后很久就会到达。
微软应该直接把这叫做网络摄像头。它会适当地设定预期。另外，这是一个很棒的网络摄像头！你可以把二人组的屏幕放在你想要的地方，不用拿着手机就可以接听电话。如果你鬼鬼祟祟的，你可以在通话时在另一个屏幕上玩Holedown的游戏。我不是那样做的。(我就是这么做的。)。
很多人可能会说Surface Duo的规格已经过时了。它拥有去年的骁龙855处理器，没有5G。我对这些事情都不感兴趣-尽管，在几年内，缺乏5G可能会是一个小麻烦。但不可否认的是，Surface Duo的1400美元价格肯定不是由于高昂的零部件成本造成的。
事实上，骁龙855的所有额外开发时间都带来了一些好处。微软表示，它能够与高通合作，将855作为稳定的目标，大幅优化其多任务系统。我相信他们的话，如果没有这样的努力，事情会更麻烦。
优化时间的一个更切实的好处是电池续航时间：相当不错！当我得知Surface Duo内部的两块电池加起来只有3577毫安时，这绝对不是我想要的。与它被要求点亮的像素数量相比，这只是一个微不足道的数字，而且明显低于许多单屏幕Android手机的像素数量。然而，我一直都是一次充电就能坚持到一天结束，待机时间也很好。
如果我对规格有什么不满的话，那就是这两款手机只有6 GB的内存，这对于普通的Android手机来说已经足够了，但这并不足以阻止应用程序在后台比我希望的更早关闭。同时可靠地运行两个应用程序而其中一个不出现问题也是不够的--Surface Duo的全部意义就是同时运行两个应用程序。
这是Surface二人组不是很划算的另一个例子-8、10甚至12 GB是售价超过1000美元的Android设备的标准配置，远低于1400美元。Pixel4A的内存与二人组相同，售价350美元。
Surface二人组试图成为一种新的设备-不是手机，也不是平板电脑，而是介于两者之间的东西。实际上，这个项目听起来有点耳熟。当史蒂夫·乔布斯(Steve Jobs)推出iPad时，他为这款设备下了赌注：
最近出现了一个问题：中间有没有第三类设备的空间？笔记本电脑和智能手机之间有什么东西吗？为了真正创造一个新的设备类别，这些设备必须在一些关键任务上做得更好。
微软不太可能通过这两家公司占据iPad大小的市场。但是，尽管有这些漏洞，我确实认为它已经证明了自己是一种新型设备-或者至少，它已经赢得了在新兴设备类别中保持一席之地的机会，这些设备试图通过某种方式将屏幕对半折叠，以便在你的口袋里容纳更大的屏幕。
但对于这两家公司来说，更好的比较不是iPad，而是最初的Surface平板电脑。当它第一次宣布的时候，它还没有真正准备好。它有一堆非常好的想法，但执行很糟糕，很多人就是不明白微软想要什么。但几年后，微软终于推出了Surface Pro 3，它实际上实现了第一款Surface想要做的事情。
这个曲面二人组？它还没有真正准备好。它有很多好点子，但有些地方的执行力很差，很多人不会得到微软想要的东西。这里有足够多的问题让我不推荐它。如果这不花1400美元，也许我们可以进行一次不同的对话。但它确实存在。如果你想花那么多钱买一部工作手机，Note 20 Ultra可以实现分屏，配有手写笔，摄像头很好，可以很好地运行所有这些微软应用程序。
但就像第一个Surface一样，Surface二人组中有很多光明和潜力。微软对移动计算新方向的愿景是我今年看到的最清晰、最强烈的，但选择方向和到达目的地仍然是两回事。
微软之前至少四次在口袋设备游戏中被淘汰-从Wince到PocketPC，从Windows Mobile到Windows Phone，这家公司对失败非常熟悉。但在二人组中，我可以看到微软从所有这些错误中吸取教训，我很高兴看到该公司再次投入战斗。我希望这首Surface二人组不是它的最后一轮。

    Microsoft’s Surface Duo aspires to be something different from any other gadget you’ve used. It could be mistaken for a phone or a small tablet, but it’s both less and more than those things.
 When it’s closed, there are no screens or cameras. In shape and appearance, it’s like a small book, a digital version of the Field Notes notebook I carry around in my back pocket. When open, you get two screens side by side or one screen with the other folded back. And like a notebook, it feels somehow more natural to hold than a phone’s vertical slab shape. You hold it in your hand with the two sides angled toward you like a book.
 In truth, there’s nothing the Surface Duo can do that you can’t do on your current smartphone or tablet. Your smartphone surely takes better photos, and your tablet doesn’t have a big gap in the middle of it. But the difference with the Surface Duo is that the  way you do things is unique. I found myself tackling tasks that would have frustrated me on those other devices.
 Microsoft is charging a starting price of $1,399 for the Surface Duo. Given its capabilities relative to other phones, it is absolutely not worth that price. And even after a couple of weeks, I’m not entirely convinced that the Surface Duo actually makes me more productive than any standard single-screen phone. But even though it doesn’t always get it right, sometimes there’s just a little less mental friction to multitasking on the Duo. I often  feel more productive with the Duo.
     The Surface Duo is a lovely, well-designed object. Every millimeter of it has clearly been crafted with intention — though sometimes that intention is so focused that it can lead to annoying compromises.
 The most important thing to know about the Duo’s hardware is its thickness: 4.8mm when open and 9.9mm when closed. Microsoft’s dedication to thinness is what makes the Duo a pocketable, ergonomic device. It’s also relatively light for a device with two screens and two batteries, at 250 grams.
 The thinness and lightness of the Surface Duo are essential to its appeal, as is the fact that Microsoft kept the outside free of screens or camera bumps. Those choices make the thing thin and comfortable to hold, but they also send a signal that this is a different kind of device.
 The feel of opening this device like a book — and shutting it like one — is fundamentally different than unlocking a smartphone. It puts you in a different mindset in both cases. The intentionality of the design encourages you to be intentional with your use of the device and to be intentional in  not using it.
  The hinge is classic Surface, which is to say it’s meticulously designed to feel great. It rotates a full 360 degrees so you can swing one of the screens around the back and use it like a traditional phone. Or you can angle one side up, and it’ll hold its place while you’re making a video call. It’s firm without being stiff, and all of those tight tolerances minimize the size of the gap between the screens.
 It’s glass on the front and back — both fronts and both backs, I guess I should say. They’re sandwiched around a metal rail. The rail houses the USB-C port but no headphone jack. For once, the argument that the phone is too thin to fit a headphone jack makes sense. It’s also where you’ll find the volume buttons, power button, and fingerprint sensor (which should be integrated into the power button, if you ask me, but it is not).
 Not everything is perfect. If I look very closely, I can see a tiny lack of symmetry at the hinge and maybe even the slightest bowing out along the top and bottom. It also ships with plastic bumper rails, which helps with the fairly sharp corners on the edges and also with grip. There’s also a very slight color variation between the two displays on my review unit — the white point on the left-hand side seems a little redder. It’s not something I noticed until reading a Kindle book with the lights turned low.
 But the white point isn’t a major complaint about the hardware. I’ll save that for the bezels above and below the dual 5.6-inch OLED screens. They’re big, and I think they’re one of those annoying compromises that were necessary to keep the Duo so thin. As with most bezels, notches, and other screen borders, you get used to them.
 Otherwise, they’re good screens. They’re readable in bright light, pixel-dense, and have good viewing angles. If you have a Surface Pen, it will work on this screen, too. They don’t have the high refresh rates that are increasingly standard on $1,000-plus phones, however.
  Those screens are also quite a bit wider than any recent smartphone. Each is designed with a 4:3 aspect ratio, which translates to a device that you can’t use with one hand — even when folded in half. Like the rest of the Duo’s design, I think that width is meant to subtly influence how you think about the device. Your apps look and feel just a tiny bit more like windows on your desktop instead of lists you end up doomscrolling.
 Many other things have been sacrificed at the altar of thinness: NFC, wireless charging, a bigger battery, expandable storage, 5G, and perhaps more modern specs like a faster processor and more RAM. There’s only a single speaker, and it’s no great shakes. But all of those things you’d expect on a phone of this price are theoretically expendable because the Surface Duo is theoretically not a phone but something else entirely.
 Delivering on that promise falls to the software, which will make or break the whole thing.
   Microsoft may have bitten off more than it can chew with the software on the Surface Duo. The original version it sent out to reviewers was riddled with show-stopping bugs and crashes. A little less than a week ago, the company pushed out a software update — the same one that will land on retail devices today — that solved an appreciable percentage of those bugs.
 But some bugs persist. There are hassles with the camera app, with lag and missed inputs, and other oddities like the keyboard jumping into view for no earthly reason. If for no other reason than software stability, it’s worth waiting to see what more people say about their Surface Duo experience over time before considering buying one.
 Surprisingly, one of those problems is  not the complaint — long-running and richly deserved — that Android tablet apps aren’t good. Because there aren’t many Android tablets, many Android developers rationally choose not to make tablet-optimized versions of their apps, which means there’s no incentive to make Android tablets, and so it goes.
 Running two apps on two screens is often easier than trying to split-screen an Android tablet
 The app availability problem can turn into a vicious spiral, a problem Microsoft knows all too well from Windows Phone. So one of the cleverest pieces of software design on the Surface Duo is simply what Microsoft chose  not to do: make a tablet. Instead, the Duo is exactly what its name suggests: two phone-sized screens meant primarily for running apps side by side.
 Just on its own, the ability to easily launch side-by-side apps is great. Without having to learn some whole other interface, you can simply tap on the icon you want on each screen and use them next to each other. That may not seem like something you’re interested in for your phone, but perhaps that’s because it’s been such a pain with a single screen.
 One of the things that helps you to be productive on your laptop is windows — the interface with boxes with your apps, I mean, not the OS. Having windows makes it easier to cross-reference stuff and less likely you’ll lose your state when you do another task. And you can move stuff across from one window to another without having to muck about with other interface elements.
 But every attempt to replicate that on phones thus far has been kind of crappy. On the Duo, it’s better simply because it’s easy to open up an app on each screen. Microsoft has tried to tout drag and drop between apps, but it’s hard to get that gesture right, and it only works in a handful of apps — so thankfully, tried-and-true copy and paste works just fine.
  The hinge allows you to contort the Duo’s screens into what Microsoft calls “postures.” There’s a tent mode for watching movies, a single screen mode with one of the screens flipped around, a mode with a big keyboard, and a full-screen mode. In full screen, most apps are oblivious to the existence of the gap between screens, and so content either looks goofily split or even gets cut off in that negative space between OLEDs.
 The Kindle app is a nice example of turning the gap into an advantage: you get a page on each side and the ability to hold the Duo like it’s a little book. But outside of that, the only apps that handle that gap well are (unsurprisingly) Microsoft’s own. Outlook, in particular, is great: you get your email list on the left side and emails on the right. Hit forward, and the original email scoots over to the left and the entire right screen becomes the compose window for your new email. This means you don’t have to constantly scroll down and up again as you comment point-by-point to an email.
 One posture I like: the big keyboard. When you rotate the Duo 90 degrees when there’s an active cursor, the bottom screen automatically turns into a keyboard while the main screen expands to the full view. It lets you see what you’re typing on a larger canvas and gives you a really big keyboard to boot.
 But getting apps into their proper place for each of these postures isn’t easy because Android’s natural state is a single phone screen. And that’s where the majority of the bugs really start cropping up.
 It’s a shame, too, because those bugs are a result of Microsoft making the  right choices when it comes to customizing Android for a true multitasking experience. I don’t think this will be obvious to the casual observer, but after spending a decade reviewing dozens of different flavors of Android, I can say this definitively: Microsoft chose one of the hardest possible roads it could in customizing Android to fit its needs, but it is the right road.
 Unlike Samsung, LG, and others who have dabbled in split-screen and folding screen versions of Android, Microsoft has tried to make a multitasking system that doesn’t seem like it’s tacked on top of Android’s more traditional interface.
  Although a lot of manufacturers are sticking with buttons, modern versions of Android work by swiping up from the bottom to get to the core parts of the OS like multitasking views, the home screen, and your apps. So Microsoft has taken that system and added to it. You can swipe up to get an app window and then drag it around on the two screens to put it in one of those modes.
 It’s a noble goal, but remembering what all the swipes do depending on your context is a huge hurdle. Sometimes a swipe brings up multitasking, sometimes it goes home, sometimes it launches the app drawer, sometimes it flings an app to the other screen if you angle it just right (or wrong). Oh, and if you turn the Duo sideways, don’t forget that up is now over on the side.
 It’s too much, but it’s a learnable system, and I eventually got proficient in it and even found myself entering that “flow” state that Surface chief Panos Panay likes to talk about. But just as often, I’d enter a rage state as the Duo did something I didn’t expect or lagged out when I was trying to position a window.
 The real shame of all of this is that Microsoft has achieved the holy grail of every other Android phone maker not named Google: a comprehensive, unified, and coherent ecosystem of apps and services. If you’re in Microsoft’s ecosystem, you will have Outlook, Office, compatibility with Your Phone on Windows 10, OneNote, and OneDrive, all working together in harmony.
 This is all miles better than whatever Samsung or LG equivalent you’ll find on their phones. Plus, Microsoft doesn’t force you to use any of it if you don’t want to. Google search is the default instead of Bing, Gmail’s sitting right there, and so on.
 Speaking of Google, I am serious when I lay some of these issues at the feet of Android itself. Google has been touting support for foldables for a couple of years now, but it’s been failing to make Android tablets successful for much longer. The result of that technical debt is that Android is woefully unprepared for devices like the Surface Duo and Samsung’s Galaxy Z Fold 2. But at the end of the day, it’s not Google’s logo on the back of the Surface Duo. It’s Microsoft’s.
 Through it all, there were still moments of feeling like I could get more done on the Duo than I could on other phones. And as time went on, those moments turned into longer stretches.
   The Surface Duo’s camera is trash. I should talk to you about its redeeming qualities, like the fact that it has a restrained opinion on color balancing that I appreciate after using Samsung’s overly vivid cameras or the fact that you can dual-screen the camera app to see a roll of your selfies on one side while you take pictures with the other. But I can’t.
  It’s likely that the camera is yet another casualty of Microsoft’s abiding dedication to thinness for the Surface Duo. Perhaps the 11-megapixel sensor is simply the best that could fit into that thin frame. But if the Google Pixel 4A has taught us anything, it’s that good software can work wonders with mediocre hardware.
 Instead, the only wonder I experienced is wondering how to get the camera to work in the first place. Since it’s placed on the inside above one of the screens, taking selfies is easy. Launch the camera, take a photo, smile at the results. Heck, Microsoft even has a passable portrait mode in good lighting conditions — well, passable by 2018 standards, anyway.
  But if you want to take a photo of somebody else, you have to rotate the other screen so it becomes a viewfinder. But when you try to do that, there’s a 50/50 chance the viewfinder will be on the wrong side. And getting it to the side is a literal exercise in frustration. You have to keep twisting and tapping the Duo until it figures it out. After all that work, the results are muddy, noisy, and in low light, they’re a mess. Plus, they arrive well after you hit the shutter button.
  Microsoft should have just called this a webcam. It would have set expectations appropriately. Plus, it’s a great webcam! You can position the Duo’s screen right where you want it and take a call without having to hold the phone. And if you’re sneaky, you can play a game of  Holedown on the other screen during your Zoom call. Not that I did that. (I did that.)
   Much will likely be made of the fact that the Surface Duo has outdated specs. It has last year’s Snapdragon 855 processor and no 5G. I don’t care about any of those things — though, in a couple of years, the lack of 5G may be a minor annoyance. But there’s no denying that the $1,400 price of the Surface Duo certainly isn’t a result of high component costs.
 In fact, all of that extra development time with the Snapdragon 855 has yielded some benefits. Microsoft says it was able to heavily optimize its multitasking system in collaboration with Qualcomm, using the 855 as a stable target. I’ll take their word for it that it would have been buggier without that effort.
  A more tangible benefit of that optimization time is battery life: it’s pretty good! That’s absolutely not what I expected when I learned that the two batteries inside the Surface Duo add up to a mere 3,577mAh. That’s a paltry number when compared to the number of pixels it’s being asked to light up, and it’s notably less than what many single-screen Android phones have. Yet, I’m consistently making it to the end of the day on a single charge, and standby time is excellent.
 If I have one complaint about the specs it’s that the Duo only has 6GB of RAM That’s plenty for your average Android phone, but it’s not enough to keep apps from closing sooner than I’d like in the background. It’s also not quite enough to reliably run two apps at once without one of them getting a hiccup — and the whole point of the Surface Duo is to run two apps at once.
 It’s another instance of the Surface Duo not being a great value — 8, 10, and even 12 gigs is the norm for Android devices that cost over a thousand dollars, much less $1,400. The Pixel 4A has the same amount of RAM as the Duo, and it costs $350.
  The Surface Duo is trying to be a new kind of device — not a phone, not a tablet, but something in between. That project sounds vaguely familiar, actually. When  Steve Jobs introduced the iPad, he set up the stakes for that device:
 The question has arisen, lately: is there room for a third category of device in the middle? Something in between the laptop and the smartphone? In order to really create a new category of devices, those devices are going to have to be far better at doing some key tasks.
 It’s unlikely that Microsoft has lit upon an iPad-sized market with the Duo. But despite the bugs, I do think it has made a case for itself as a new kind of device — or at the very least, it has earned a chance to keep a spot in the emerging class of devices that try to fit a larger screen in your pocket by folding it in half in some way.
 But a better comparison for the Duo isn’t the iPad; it’s the original Surface tablet. When it was first announced, it wasn’t really ready. It had a bunch of very good ideas, but the execution was bad, and a lot of people just didn’t get what Microsoft was going for. But a few years later, Microsoft finally came through with the Surface Pro 3, which actually achieved what the first Surface was trying to do.
  This Surface Duo? It’s not really ready. It has a bunch of good ideas, but the execution is bad in places, and a lot of people aren’t going to get what Microsoft is going for. There are more than enough problems here to keep me from recommending it. Maybe if this didn’t cost fourteen hundred bucks we could be having a different conversation. But it does. And if you want to spend that much on a work phone, the Note 20 Ultra does split-screen, comes with a stylus, has a good camera, and runs all of those Microsoft apps just fine.
 But like that first Surface, there are more than a few glimmers of vision and potential in the Surface Duo. Microsoft has the clearest, strongest vision for a new direction in mobile computing that I’ve seen this year, but picking a direction and getting to the destination are still two different things.
 Microsoft has gotten knocked out of the pocketable device games at least four times before — from WinCE to PocketPC to Windows Mobile to Windows Phone, this company is intimately familiar with failure. But in the Duo, I can see Microsoft learning from all those mistakes, and I’m glad to see the company back in the fight again. I hope that this first Surface Duo isn’t its last round.

数以亿计的消费者在日常生活中使用亚马逊Alexa或苹果Siri等语音助手完成简单的任务。现在，一家公司想把会说话的机器人带到下一个层次：高度对话的客户服务领域。
以Blade Runner的基因工程人类命名的Replicant，正凭借其软件进入未知领域，该软件结合了基于文本的聊天机器人和客服代表的实时AI助手。周四，这家初创公司宣布，它已经为其自主呼叫中心筹集了2700万美元的首轮A轮融资。这笔融资由Norwest Venture Partners牵头，最近加入了保险公司State Farm的风险部门，以及原子能公司、彭博贝塔公司(Bloomberg Beta)和科斯塔诺亚风险投资公司(Costanoa Ventures)，后者去年参与了700万美元的种子期融资。Replicant不愿透露加薪后的估值，但指出Norwest合伙人斯科特·比丘克(Scott Beechuk)将加入董事会，他曾担任Salesforce高级副总裁，负责公司的客户服务平台。
首席执行官加迪·沙米亚在谈到他的人工智能时说：“我们不会假装是人类，但这种体验与与特工交谈非常相似。”“可以把这想象成自动驾驶--(人类)代理只是个异常处理者，他们不必处理整个呼叫流程。”
这家总部位于旧金山的公司创造了一种人工智能，可以用一种像人类一样的对话声音在电话上与人交谈。机器人利用深度学习来理解人类句子的错综复杂之处，并可以完全解决某些客户服务查询(更复杂的问题会被路由给人类代理)。它为那些本来可能会被挂断的人节省了时间，沙米亚说，快速的处理时间进一步允许机器人自己将通话时长缩短一半。“没有嗯和啊的，”他说。“我们不需要花30秒来读取文件。”
Replicant成立于2017年，由原子公司创立，该公司是远程医疗服务Hims和租赁平台Bungalow等公司背后的风险投资基金和初创公司建设者。沙米亚在客服软件制造商TalkDesk担任过一段时间的首席运营官后于2019年加入，但Replicant将他与首席技术官本杰明·格莱茨曼(Benjamin Gleitzman)和原子公司联合创始人杰克·亚伯拉罕(Jack Abraham)一起视为联合创始人。理由：格莱茨曼和亚伯拉罕开发了训练机器与人类对话的技术，而沙米亚则引导人工智能进入客户服务领域。
根据Forrester的数据，呼叫中心是一个带来150亿美元收入的行业，它受益于自动化和人工智能的创新，特别是基于文本的聊天机器人，它可以将简单的任务从客服人员手中转移出去，最近又通过可以处理对话并向客服人员实时提供建议响应或相关资源的“增强智能”软件。沙米亚认为，Replicant的技术消除了呼叫中心代表必须回答的大量对话，从而大大提高了生产率。
他说：“通过增加代理，我在三到四年前对其进行了研究，认为建造良好它的成本和复杂性与建造Replicant之类的东西的成本和复杂性相似，但其价值可能是5%。”
沙米亚说，现在每月有200万个客户服务电话通过Replicant。该公司拥有20多家企业客户，其中包括企业餐厅的技术堆栈Xenial，该公司在新冠肺炎疫情开始时聘请Replicant作为其客户服务技术解决方案。汉堡王(Burger King)和温迪(Wendy‘s)等连锁店的员工如果有问题要与公司总部沟通，比如收银机坏了，可以与Replicant的语音人工智能交谈，语音人工智能也已集成到一个平台中，以接受客户订单。沙米亚说，收入“数以百万计”，自今年年初以来已经增长了15倍。
A系列的资金将用于投放市场，以及加快研发，即提高语音AI的准确性，并解决更多的总呼叫流。但是，尽管Replicant声称它可以自动化部分客户服务呼叫，但Shamia表示，该公司并没有真正担心人工智能会取代人们的工作。他说：“自工业革命以来，技术总体上一直在改变工作。”“人工智能取代了任务，但不是工作。”他认为，Replicant为客户服务代表做的事情就像自动取款机为银行出纳员做的那样--减少了花在平凡任务上的时间。
“我不认为Replicant会成为一个完全自主的呼叫中心，”沙米亚说。他说：“我们将始终与代理商合作，我们将始终与企业合作。我们的目标将是能够接听越来越多这类平凡的电话，或许未来还能接手其他渠道。“
note_en:
Hundreds of millions of consumers use voice assistants such as Amazon Alexa or Apple’s Siri for simple tasks in their daily lives. Now, one company wants to take talking bots to the next level: the highly conversational field of customer service.
  Replicant, named after  Blade Runner’s genetically engineering humans, is entering uncharted territory with its software that works as a combination between text-based chatbots and real-time AI assistants for customer service reps. On Thursday, the startup announced that it has raised a $27 million Series A funding round for its autonomous call center. The financing was led by Norwest Venture Partners and newly joined by insurance firm State Farm’s venture arm, alongside Atomic, Bloomberg Beta and Costanoa Ventures which participated in the $7 million seed round last year. Replicant would not reveal its valuation after the raise, but notes that Norwest partner Scott Beechuk, a former Salesforce senior vice president in charge of the company’s customer service platform, will join the board of directors.
  “We don’t pretend to be human, but the experience is pretty similar to speaking with an agent,” says CEO Gadi Shamia of his artificial intelligence. “Think of this as autopilot—the [human] agent is just the exception handler, they don’t have to deal with the entire call flow.”
     The San Francisco-based company has created AI that can talk to people over the phone with a conversational and human-like voice. The bot employs deep learning to understand the intricacies of humans’ sentences, and can fully resolve certain customer service inquiries (more complex issues are routed to a human agent). It saves time for people who might otherwise be put on hold, and Shamia says the speedy processing time further allows the bot to cut the length of calls themselves in half. “There’s no um’s and ah’s,” he says. “We don’t have to spend 30 seconds to read the file.”
  Replicant was founded in 2017 out of Atomic, the venture capital fund and startup builder behind companies like telemedicine service Hims and rental platform Bungalow. Shamia joined in 2019 after a stint as chief operating officer for customer service software maker Talkdesk, but Replicant counts him as a cofounder alongside chief technology officer Benjamin Gleitzman and Jack Abraham, cofounder of Atomic. The rationale: Gleitzman and Abraham developed the technology to train machines to talk to humans, while Shamia was the one to guide the AI into the customer service space.
    Call centers, a sector that brings in $15 billion in revenue according to Forrester, have benefitted from innovations in automation and artificial intelligence, notably with text-based chatbots that reroute simple tasks away from customer service agents, and more recently through  “augmented intelligence” software that can process conversations and provide suggested responses or relevant resources to the agent in real time. Shamia thinks Replicant’s technology provides a massive leap in productivity by eliminating a large swath of conversations that call center reps have to answer.
  “With augmenting the agent, I looked into it three to four years ago and decided that the cost and complexity of building it well is similar to the cost and complexity of building something like Replicant—but the value is maybe 5%,” he says.
  Two million customer service calls per month now go through Replicant, Shamia says. The company has more than 20 enterprise customers, including Xenial, a technology stack for enterprise restaurants which enlisted Replicant as its customer service tech solution at the start of the Covid-19 pandemic. Employees for chains such as Burger King and Wendy’s talk to Replicant’s voice AI if they have issues to field with corporate headquarters, such as a broken cash register, and the voice AI has also been integrated into a platform to take customer orders. Revenue is “in the millions,” says Shamia, and has increased 15-fold since the start of the year.
  The capital from the Series A will go towards go-to-market efforts, as well as accelerating research and development, namely increasing the voice AI’s accuracy and resolving more of the total call flow. But though Replicant claims it can automate away a portion of all customer service calls, Shamia says the company is not making true fears that AI will replace people’s jobs. “Technology in general changes jobs all the time, ever since the Industrial Revolution,” he says. “AI replaces tasks, but not jobs.” Replicant, he suggests, is doing for customer service reps what ATMs did for bank tellers—reducing the time spent on mundane tasks.
  “I don’t foresee Replicant to be a completely autonomous call center,” Shamia says. “We’re always going to partner with agents, and we’re always going to partner with businesses. Our goal will be being able to take more and more of these mundane calls, and maybe taking over other channels in the future.”
title: Replicant是一家使用基于文本的聊天机器人和人工智能助手来帮助人类代表的客户服务平台，由Norwest Venture Partners牵头筹集了2700万美元的首轮融资
title_en: Replicant, a customer service platform that uses text-based chatbots and AI assistants to help human reps, raises $27M Series A led by Norwest Venture Partners

北美最大的网络保险服务提供商之一Coalition今天发布的一份报告显示，在2020年上半年提交的网络保险索赔中，勒索软件事件占到了41%。
大量的索赔证实了之前来自多家网络安全公司的报告，即勒索软件是当今最普遍和最具破坏性的威胁之一。
勒索软件不会按行业进行歧视。Coalition补充说，我们已经看到我们服务的几乎每个行业的赎金袭击事件都在增加。
该公司补充说，仅在2020年上半年，我们观察到投保人遭受勒索软件攻击的频率增加了260%，平均赎金需求增加了47%。
在最咄咄逼人的团伙中，这家网络保险公司列出了Maze和DoupelPaymer，这两家公司最近开始从被黑客攻击的网络中窃取数据，并威胁要公布专门泄密网站的数据，这是双重敲诈勒索计划的一部分。
根据2020年上半年面临勒索软件攻击的客户提交的网络保险索赔，Coalition表示，迷宫勒索软件团伙是最贪婪的，该团伙要求的赎金要求是整体平均水平的6倍。
但Coalition表示，除了勒索软件事件，它还记录了因资金转移欺诈攻击和商业电子邮件泄露(BEC)事件而提交的网络保险索赔数量激增，从2019年到2020年，第一起增长了35%，第二起增长了67%。
这两起事件都是类似类型的事件，犯罪团伙欺骗公司向攻击者控制的账户付款。不同的是，资金转账欺诈攻击也可以通过电话或邮件进行；BEC攻击纯粹是通过电子邮件进行的。
据报道，这些事件造成的损失从每起事件的几千美元到远高于100万美元不等，但Coalition表示，使用Microsoft Office 365的公司发生的BEC事件是使用其他类型电子邮件提供商的组织的3.2倍。
尽管如此，Coalition表示，在许多资金转移欺诈攻击以及BEC攻击中，损失的资金可以通过快速干预来追回。
&#34；自2018年初以来，[...]。这家网络保险公司表示：在所有案件中，我们能够追回55%的资金，我们已经为这些客户追回了84%的损失资金。
note_en:
Ransomware incidents have accounted for 41% of cyber insurance claims filed in the first half of 2020, according to a report published today by  Coalition, one of the largest providers of cyber insurance services in North America.
 The high number of claims comes to confirm previous reports from multiple cyber-security firms that ransomware is one of today's most prevalent and destructive threats.
 "Ransomware doesn't discriminate by industry. We've seen an increase in ransom attacks across almost every industry we serve," Coalition added.
 "In the first half of 2020 alone, we observed a 260% increase in the frequency of ransomware attacks amongst our policyholders, with the average ransom demand increasing 47%," the company added.
 Among the most aggressive gangs, the cyber insurer listed Maze and DoppelPaymer, which have recently begun exfiltrating data from hacked networks, and threatening to release data on  specialized leak sites, as part of double extortion schemes.
 Based on cyber insurance claims filed by customers who faced a ransomware attack in the first half of 2020, Coalition said the Maze ransomware gang was the most greedy, with the group requesting ransom demands six times larger than the overall average.
  But besides ransomware incidents, Coalition said it also recorded a spike in the number of cyber insurance claims filed for funds transfer fraud attacks and business email compromise (BEC) events, with the first growing 35% from 2019 to 2020, and the second growing 67%.
   Both are similar types of incidents, where criminal gangs trick a company into making a payment into an attacker-controlled account. The difference is that funds transfer fraud attacks can also occur via phone call or mail; BEC attacks are carried out purely via email.
 Reported losses from these incidents have ranged from the low thousands to well above $1 million per event, but Coalition says that companies using Microsoft Office 365 have seen 3.2 times more BEC incidents than organizations using other types of email providers.
 Nonetheless, Coalition said that in many cases of funds transfer fraud attacks, as well as BEC attacks, lost funds could be recovered, with quick intervention.
 "Since the beginning of 2018, [...] we've been able to recover funds in 55% of all cases, and we've recovered 84% of lost funds for these clients," the cyber insurer said.
title: 报告发现，2020年上半年提交的网络保险索赔中，勒索软件事件占41%，平均赎金需求增加47%

note_en:
Back in June, our colleagues at   9to5Mac discovered in the iOS 13.5 beta that Apple was planning to bundle their services into a single subscription. With the latest update to the Apple Music app for Android, we can now confirm that a unified “Apple One” subscription should be coming soon.
  About APK Insight: In this “APK Insight” post, we’ve decompiled the latest version of an application that has been uploaded to the Google Play Store. When we decompile these files (called APKs, in the case of Android apps), we’re able to see various lines of code within that hint at possible future features. Keep in mind that Apple may or may not ever ship these features, and our interpretation of what they are may be imperfect. We’ll try to enable those that are closer to being finished, however, to show you how they’ll look in the case that they do ship. With that in mind, read on.
  Last month,  Bloomberg reported  new details about this subscription bundle, such as the services that will be included. Reportedly, there may be a base bundle with just Apple TV+ and Apple Music as well as more premium bundles that include Apple Arcade, Apple News+, and more iCloud storage. At that time, the name “Apple One” was tossed around as a working name.
 With the  latest release of Apple Music, version 3.4.0 beta, we find that Apple may have settled on the name “Apple One,” alongside an internal codename “aristotle.” These new strings in the app all but confirm that Apple Music will be included with Apple One when it launches.
   Interestingly, Apple has clear messaging in place that Apple One and your existing Apple Music subscription will not overlap, ensuring you won’t double pay.
 <string name=”aristotle_main_more_info”>Your Apple Music subscription will be included in Apple One starting %s. You will not be charged for both subscriptions.</string>
 Notably, it seems you will not be able to manage or renew your Apple One subscription from the Android version of the Apple Music app. Instead, it seems you may need to use an iOS, macOS, or tvOS device to do it.
 <string name=”aristotle_renewaloption_subtext”>You can manage your Apple One subscription using your iPhone, iPad, Apple TV or Mac.</string>
 Unfortunately, no other details could be found in the app, such as pricing or when Apple plans to launch this new subscription bundle. The next possible opportunity for Apple One to launch would be  at next week’s Apple event, currently predicted to bring new offerings in the iPad Air and Apple Watch lines.
  You’re reading 9to5Google — experts who break news about Google and its surrounding ecosystem, day after day. Be sure to check out  our homepage for all the latest news, and follow 9to5Google on  Twitter,  Facebook, and  LinkedIn to stay in the loop. Don’t know where to start? Check out our  exclusive stories,  reviews,  how-tos, and  subscribe to our YouTube channel

谷歌高管在今天的一次在线新闻发布会上以及在一篇博客文章中概述了这些变化。最大的变化似乎是围绕自动完成功能的政策，谷歌根据你到目前为止键入的内容来推荐搜索。
该公司表示，现在将删除任何似乎支持或反对候选人或政党的自动完成预测，或者对投票或选举过程发表声明的预测。这将意味着取消“你可以通过电话投票”、“你不能通过电话投票”或任何建议你向政党或候选人捐款的预测。
与此同时，谷歌强调，这只适用于AutoComplete。用户仍然可以搜索关于投票或候选人的信息-只是不会自动看到这些查询，候选人或政党使用自动完成功能来驱动用户进行所需的搜索应该会变得更加困难。
谷歌全球政策和标准高级总监大卫·格拉夫(David Graff)表示，这只是公司现有政策的延伸，“与其说是一种全新的政策或哲学方法，不如说是一种全新的政策或哲学方法。”
他补充说，“我们敏锐地意识到，随着即将到来的选举，…。人们有强烈的意见，考虑到COVID的背景，关于投票信息以及在大流行的背景下会如何发挥作用，有很多问题。“。
格拉夫还称这是一种“保守”的做法，一些无伤大雅的建议可能会被删除，这样谷歌就不会冒着允许选举期间的错误信息潜入的风险。
谷歌搜索质量团队负责人潘杜·纳亚克也表示，这项政策将使绝大多数的自动补全建议“完全不受影响”。
他说：“当然，他们得到了超大的讨论，但实际上他们只是自动补全的一小部分。”
谷歌今天强调的其他变化和进展包括成立了一个情报部门，这是一个由分析师组成的团队，负责监控新闻并识别“潜在的信息威胁”；谷歌现在可以在几分钟内识别突发新闻时刻，而几年前只需要40分钟；新的程序旨在防止对维基百科的破坏潜入搜索结果旁边的知识图谱面板；以及谷歌图片中的事实核查标签。
note_en:
Google executives outlined these changes at an online press event today, as well as in  a blog post. The biggest change seems to be in its policies around Autocomplete, the feature where Google suggests a search based on what you’ve typed so far.
 The company says that it will now remove any Autocomplete predictions that seem to endorse or oppose a candidate or a political party, or that make a claim about voting or the electoral process. That would mean eliminating predictions like “you can vote by phone,” “you can’t vote by phone” or anything suggesting that you donate to a party or candidate.
  At the same time, Google emphasized that this only applies to Autocomplete. Users will still be able to search for information around voting or candidates — you just won’t see those queries automatically, and it should become much harder for a candidate or party to use Autocomplete to drive users to make a desirable search.
 David Graff, Google’s senior director of global policy and standards, said this is merely an extension of the company’s existing policies, and “not so much a completely new policy or philosophical approach.”
  He added, “We’re acutely aware that with this upcoming election … people have strong opinions and given the backdrop of COVID, there’s a lot of questions about voting information and how that might play out against the backdrop of the pandemic.”
 Graff also described this as a “conservative” approach, one in which some innocuous suggestions will probably be eliminated so that Google doesn’t risk allowing misinformation around the election from sneaking in.
 Pandu Nayak, who heads Google’s search quality team, also said this policy will leave the vast majority of Autocomplete suggestions “completely untouched.”
 “They get an outsized discussion around them, of course, but they’re actually a very small fraction of Autocompletes,” he said.
 The other changes and progress that Google is highlighting today include the formation of an Intelligence Desk, a team of analysts that monitors the news and identifies “potential information threats”; the fact that Google can now identify breaking news moments in just a few minutes, compared to 40 minutes a few years ago; new programs designed to prevent vandalism on Wikipedia from sneaking onto the Knowledge Graph panels that appear alongside search results; and fact-check labels in Google Images.

title_en: Google says it will remove any Autocomplete suggestions that endorse or oppose a candidate or a party, or make a claim about voting or the electoral process

华盛顿(CNN商业报道)推特周四宣布，它正在扩大针对选举相关错误信息的政策，设定新的规则，这可能会迫使该平台在2020年竞选的最后几个月更积极地核实唐纳德·特朗普总统的事实。
这家社交媒体巨头在一篇博文中推出了这些新政策，称推特(Twitter)将要么在包含虚假或误导性信息的推文上添加事实核查标签，要么完全隐藏包含导致选举规则混乱的虚假或误导性信息，或者发布含有未经核实的选举舞弊信息的帖子。
推特漏洞百出、主观的政策使得特朗普能够向数百万美国人传播源源不断的选举错误信息。该公司在5月份指责特朗普发布了一条误导性的推文，为大型科技公司走在了前列，但那个分水岭时刻最终看起来更像是一个局外人。推特很少给特朗普的推文贴上事实核查标签，这些推文包含关于投票的虚假信息，目前还不清楚贴标签能起到多大作用。
Twitter表示，新规定将于下周生效，明确禁止特朗普倾向于发布的许多材料，这让该公司在试图帮助引导国家度过前所未有的投票和选举后过程的同时，与特朗普发生了冲突。
新规则包括旨在减少选举后潜在混乱的政策，由于特朗普的言辞和邮寄选票的涌入，这是今年的一个主要担忧，这将减缓计票速度。推特现在将禁止关于选举结果的误导性声明，禁止过早宣布胜利，禁止煽动阻碍权力和平交接的非法行为。
该公司表示，将对违反规定的帖子贴上标签或将其删除，但并未详细说明将采用什么程序来确定哪些内容足够恶劣，应该删除而不是贴标签。
就在上周，特朗普鼓励支持者投票两次--一次是通过邮件，另一次是当面投票，这违反了现有的推特规则，作为测试反欺诈措施的一种方式。Twitter隐藏了这些帖子，而Facebook则添加了警告标签，但两人都没有指出两次投票是非法的。
根据Twitter执行新规则的严格程度，这可能会给Facebook带来更大压力，Facebook因处理总统提供的错误信息的方式而受到批评。
纽约大学(New York University)社交媒体与政治中心(Center For Social Media And Politics)联席主任约书亚·塔克(Joshua Tucker)表示，难题在于，当这些平台执行任何形式的内容审查政策时，都害怕被指责为政治偏见。如果大型科技公司疏远了政治光谱中的一方，它们将损失惨重。在某一时刻，另一方将掌权。
社交媒体公司一直在努力应对一位热衷于分享谣言、未经证实的病毒式视频和阴谋论的总统。Facebook和twitter今年早些时候承诺打击虚假信息，但他们在夏天对欺骗性编辑的政治视频和关于新冠肺炎的有害帖子的反应往往是缺乏的，而且反应太慢。
推特表示，新的规则需要反映2020年人们投票方式的变化情况。由于这场流行病，预计将有创纪录数量的美国人投邮寄选票，这是一种安全的投票方式，在许多州都得到了成功的使用。
但邮寄投票的突然上升也给特朗普提供了制造混乱的机会。他已经做到了这一点--通常是在推特上--通过散布邮寄投票具有大规模欺诈性的虚假声明，并先发制人地质疑结果的有效性。民主党提名人乔·拜登(Joe Biden)公开质疑，如果特朗普输掉大选，他是否会和平离开白宫。
Twitter的一名发言人拒绝透露，根据新规则，特朗普之前发布的这种性质的推文是否有资格进行事实核查。仔细分析这些规则--包括宽泛的禁令和不可接受的内容的具体例子--就会发现，特朗普在过去几个月里关于选举的许多推文都违反了推特的新规则。
Twitter的新政策可能会让该公司与特朗普发生冲突，每当科技公司批准他的推文时，特朗普经常猛烈抨击他们。今年早些时候，在Twitter首次对特朗普进行事实核查后，他签署了一项基本上是象征性的行政命令，针对的是社交媒体公司，他敦促议员们通过更严格的互联网法律。
特朗普周二批评推特，公开质疑为什么该平台没有审查多数党参议员米奇·麦康奈尔(Mitch McConnell)的照片，这张照片被篡改，让他看起来像是穿着苏联军装。特朗普在推特上写道，在有偏见的大科技公司阻止你之前，停止它们！
错误信息在Twitter和Facebook上广泛传播，Facebook上周宣布了自己的计划，将如何处理今年秋天关于选举的误导性帖子。
Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格宣布，该公司不会在2020年竞选的最后一周接受新的政治广告，该公司将在有关选举的错误信息上贴上标签，包括声称人们如果去投票站可能会感染冠状病毒。
因此，Facebook将特朗普上周关于双重投票的误导性帖子贴上了标签。该公司最初增加了一个样板标签，告诉人们浏览经过认证的选举资源。但在经历了一些反弹之后，Facebook更新了标签语言，称邮寄投票在美国有着悠久的可信度历史，预计今年也会有同样的历史。
Facebook的新政策仍然允许政客们在选举日播放包含谎言的广告。即使今年的账簿上似乎有严格的规定，Twitter也一再拒绝对可能破坏公众对选举信心的明目张胆的虚假错误信息采取行动。
这些弱点和漏洞体现了科技公司试图达到的平衡。
这一次，Twitter表示，它特别关注的不仅是来自积极干预最近选举的外国行为者的威胁，还有来自美国人的威胁。
我们不会允许我们的服务在公民程序中被滥用，最重要的是选举。该公司周四表示，任何试图这样做的尝试--无论是外国的还是国内的--都将受到我们规则的严格执行，这些规则对每个人都是平等而明智的。--CNN Business多尼·O·沙利文(Donie O&39；Sullivan)对这篇报道做出了贡献。
note_en:
Washington (CNN Business)Twitter announced Thursday that it is  expanding its policies against election-related misinformation, setting new rules that will likely force the platform to more aggressively fact-check President Donald Trump during the final months of the 2020 campaign.
 The social media giant rolled out the new policies in a blog post, which said that  Twitter  (  TWTR) will either add fact-check labels to or hide altogether tweets that contain "false or misleading information that causes confusion" about election rules, or posts with "unverified information about election rigging."
 Twitter's  porous and subjective policies have enabled Trump to spread a steady stream of misinformation about the election to millions of Americans. The company led the way for Big Tech when it  rebuked Trump for a misleading tweet in May, but that watershed moment has ended up looking more like an outlier. Twitter only rarely applies fact-check labels to Trump's tweets containing false information about voting, and it's unclear how much labeling achieves.
   The new rules, which Twitter says will go into effect next week, explicitly prohibit a lot of the material Trump is prone to posting, putting the company on a collision course with Trump while it tries to help steer the country through an unprecedented voting and post-election process.
 The new rules include policies geared toward reducing  potential post-election chaos, a major concern this year because of Trump's rhetoric and the influx of mail-in ballots, which will slow down vote-counting. Twitter will now prohibit "misleading claims about the results," premature claims of victory, or "inciting unlawful conduct" that prevents a peaceful transition of power.
   The company says it will "label or remove" posts that break the rules, but didn't spell out what process will be used to determine what is egregious enough to get removed instead of labeled.
 Just last week, Trump ran afoul of existing Twitter rules when he  encouraged supporters to vote twice -- once by mail and again in-person, as a way of testing anti-fraud measures. Twitter hid the posts, while Facebook added a warning label, but neither noted that voting twice is illegal.
 Depending on how strictly Twitter implements the new rules, it could put more pressure on Facebook, which has been criticized for how it handles misinformation from the President.
 "The conundrum is that the platforms are terrified of being accused of political bias, when they enforce any sort of content moderation policy," said Joshua Tucker, co-director of the Center for Social Media and Politics at New York University. "The huge tech companies have a lot to lose if they alienate one side of the political spectrum. At some point, the other side will be in power."
  Social media companies have struggled with how to handle a President who eagerly shares rumors, unverified viral videos, and conspiracy theories. Facebook and Twitter promised earlier this year to crack down on misinformation, but their responses over the summer to deceptively edited political videos and harmful posts about Covid-19 were often lacking -- and too slow.
 Twitter says the new rules were needed to reflect "the changing circumstances of how people will vote in 2020." Because of the pandemic, a historic number of Americans are expected to cast mail-in ballots, a safe method of voting that has been used successfully in many states.
 But the sudden uptick in mail-in voting also gives Trump an opening to create chaos. He has already done so -- often on Twitter -- by spreading false claims that mail-in voting is massively fraudulent, and by preemptively questioning the validity of the results. Democratic nominee Joe Biden has openly questioned whether Trump will peacefully leave the White House if he loses.
  A Twitter spokesman declined to say whether previous Trump tweets of this nature would have qualified for fact-checking under the new rules. A close analysis of the rules -- which include broad prohibitions and specific examples of unacceptable material -- suggests that many of  Trump's tweets about the election in the past few months would violate Twitter's new rules.
 The new policies from Twitter could put the company on a collision course with Trump, who often lashes out against tech companies whenever they sanction his tweets. Earlier this year, after Twitter fact-checked Trump for the first time, he signed a  largely symbolic executive order that targeted social media companies, and he urged lawmakers to pass stricter Internet laws.
 Trump criticized Twitter on Tuesday, publicly questioning why the platform didn't censor an image of Senator Majority Mitch McConnell that was doctored to make him look like he was wearing a Soviet military uniform. Trump tweeted, "Stop biased Big Tech before they stop you!"
  Misinformation spreads widely on Twitter and Facebook alike, and Facebook announced its own plans last week for how it will deal with misleading posts about the election this fall.
 Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg announced that the company  won't accept new political ads during the final week of the 2020 campaign, and that the company will label misinformation about the election, including claims that people could get the coronavirus if they go to the polls.
 As a result, Facebook labeled  Trump's misleading post last week about double voting. The company initially added a boilerplate label telling people to browse authenticated election resources. But after some backlash, Facebook updated the label language to say, "Voting by mail has a long history of trustworthiness in the US and the same is predicted this year."
 Facebook's new policies still allow politicians to run ads containing lies through Election Day. And even with seemingly strict rules on the books this year, Twitter repeatedly declined to take actions against brazenly false misinformation that could undermine public faith in the election.
 These weak spots and loopholes exemplified the balance that tech firms are trying to strike.
   This time around, Twitter says it's specifically on the lookout for threats coming not just from foreign actors who have aggressively meddled in recent elections, but also from Americans.
 "We will not permit our service to be abused around civic processes, most importantly elections. Any attempt to do so -- both foreign and domestic -- will be met with strict enforcement of our rules, which are applied equally and judiciously for everyone," the company said on Thursday.    -- CNN Business' Donie O'Sullivan contributed to this report.
title: 推特扩大选举错误信息政策，禁止对结果的误导性声明，“煽动非法行为”以防止权力和平移交，更多
title_en: Twitter expands election misinfo policies, prohibiting misleading claims about results, “inciting unlawful conduct” to prevent peaceful transfer of power, more

更新，9月10日(美国东部时间晚上9：00)：苹果公司表示，它从来没有计划取消对堡垒之夜登录苹果的支持。
